entry.title: Re: Scheduling dates in PP - Lead time vs Basic date scheduling - Planned order vs Production order
entry.content: Nice Blog Keep up the good work

entry.title: Re: Functional Area Assignment Limitation at WBS Level Du...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud the Functional Area that you load via the Migrate Your Data – Enterprise Project migration object is maintained at the project header level and propagates (‘inherits’) to all WBS elements below it. It is standard system behavior that all WBS elements in the same subtree share the same Functional Area as the project or billing WBS element. There is no supported way in the standard migration template to assign different Functional Areas per individual WBS under the same project.

3486425- After migrating an Enterprise Project, the Profit Center on the WBS Element is showing incorrectly in app Project Planning
https://me.sap.com/notes/3486425

Best regards, 

Tiago 
SAP Technical Support

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
Welcome to the blog post for asset management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release! This release introduces significant innovations in maintenance management, designed to enhance productivity for maintenance experts, improve safety and compliance protocols, and provide more actionable, centralized data.

Below, you’ll find a curated selection of release highlights from our core product management team. Before diving in, I invite you to watch the release highlights video, featuring a digital version of me! 😀

 

Table of Contents

New Asset Overview SAP Fiori app (+demo) 
Permit to work: new scope item (7Z4) (+demo) 
Enhancements to the Manage Maintenance Notification app (+demo) 
Further innovations 
SAP Asset Performance Management 
More information/additional links

New Asset Overview SAP Fiori app

To address the challenge of navigating multiple screens to gather asset information, the 2602 release introduces the Asset Overview app. This SAP Fiori application provides a single, unified interface for maintenance experts to view and interact with the complete hierarchy of their enterprise assets. Access to this application requires EAM - Asset Overview (SAP_EAM_BC_AOVW_MNG_PC) business catalog, which is included in role templates such as Maintenance Planner (SAP_BR_MAINTENANCE_PLANNER), Maintenance Technician (SAP_BR_MAINTENANCE_TECHNICIAN), and Maintenance Supervisor (SAP_BR_MAINTENANCE_SUPERVISOR).

Key features and capabilities:

Unified hierarchy visualization: The app offers a comprehensive top-down and bottom-up view of your asset structure, combining functional locations and the equipment installed within them into a single, traversable tree. Users can expand individual nodes or the entire hierarchy to its lowest level with a single click. A powerful search function allows users to isolate a specific asset and display its complete branch up to the top-level parent.

The new app aims to provide asset-centric data access, focused on:

  • Flexible column layout: This feature allows a user to select an asset in the hierarchy on the left-hand panel and view its detailed information on the right-hand panel without leaving the main screen.
  • Master and transactional data: The detail view displays crucial master data, such as manufacturer and model number, alongside visual indicators for warranty status (e.g., color-coded for active, expired or not maintained). It also provides direct access to related transactional data, including open maintenance notifications and orders associated with the asset.

Integrated maintenance actions: The app is not just for viewing data; it enables immediate action. Users can create maintenance notifications directly from the asset detail page through two methods:

  • Quick create: A simple form requiring a description to generate a notification.
  • Joule, SAP’s AI Copilot: Users can leverage the integrated digital assistant, Joule, to create a notification using natural language prompts without leaving the app.

Learn more watching the demo below:

 

Permit to work: new scope item (7Z4)

The 2602 release introduces the Permit to Work functionality under scope item 7Z4. This feature provides a standardized process for issuing permits for high-risk maintenance activities, ensuring work is performed in a safe, secure, and compliant manner. It is particularly critical for industries such as manufacturing, oil and gas, mining, and utilities.

At its core, the Permit to Work process serves as a formal authorization system. It's designed to foster close collaboration between your maintenance, operations, and safety teams, ensuring everyone is aligned on the required precautions to minimize risks.

Initially, this scope item is available for Germany and the US. Availability for other countries will follow in subsequent updates, with a target for all standard countries by the 2702 release.

Prerequisite scope items include Reactive Maintenance (4HH), Proactive Maintenance (4H), Operational and Overhead Maintenance (4BT), and Maintenance Resource Scheduling (4WM).

Core components and configuration

New applications: This functionality is delivered with two new SAP Fiori apps:

  • Manage Work Permit Template (F4692): Allows for the creation of reusable templates with pre-defined information. This reduces the time required to create new permits and ensures that minimum safety and data standards are consistently met.
  • Manage Work Permits (F6579): The primary application for creating, processing, and managing the lifecycle of individual work permits. Permits can be created with reference to a maintenance order, an existing permit, or a pre-configured template.

Process overview

1. Initiation: A planner identifies a maintenance activity that requires a permit and initiates its creation, selecting a permit type such as hot work, cold work, or confined space.
2. Approval: The permit is submitted for review. In the initial 2602 release, the permit is automatically set to a "Ready for Issue" status. Flexible workflow capabilities are planned for future releases.
3. Issuance: An authorized user, such as a supervisor, issues the permit to the designated technician.
4. Execution and return: The technician completes the work and returns the permit to the issuer.
5. Closure: The issuer verifies the work site, normalizes the technical object, and formally closes the permit in the system. The system supports actions to "retain and close" or "retain and deactivate" the permit if work needs to be suspended and resumed later.

 Learn more watching the demo below by Ebin Joseph, from product management:

 

Enhancements to the Manage Maintenance Notification app

The Manage Maintenance Notification (F5777) app has been updated with several new features to improve usability and provide deeper insights.

Situation handling: The app now uses situation handling to deliver instant alerts to the maintenance team when a new notification is created or a key status is updated. For example, an alert can be triggered when a notification is automatically generated due to a condition-based maintenance threshold breach, enabling a proactive response.

Advanced filtering: The list report now includes "Asset Location" and "Sort Field" as filter criteria. This allows users to more effectively narrow down the list of notifications to the specific assets or areas for which they are responsible.

Visual charts for measurement documents: For notifications generated from a measurement document threshold breach, the header now includes a chart. This provides a clear visual representation of the measurement readings against their defined thresholds, allowing for quicker analysis.

Reference order tracking: A "Reference Order" field has been added as a filter and a column in the list report. This enables maintenance teams to easily identify and track all notifications that were created as a follow-on work request from a technician in the field.

Conversational AI: Similar to the Asset Overview app, users can now create maintenance notifications directly within this app using natural language conversations with Joule AI Copilot.

 Learn more watching the demo below by Syed Kader Abdul Wahab, from product management:

 

Further innovations

Perform Maintenance Jobs app - Long text formatting: Clarity in work instructions is critical for maintenance technicians. Long text fields for maintenance orders and jobs can now be formatted. This enhancement allows for the use of bolding, bullet points, and other formatting options to structure text, which improves the readability and organization of detailed instructions and notes.

Manage Maintenance Plans app - Deletion of sort fields: To improve data quality and efficiency, the Define Sort Fields for Maintenance Plans configuration activity now includes a delete option. You can now remove outdated or invalid customized sort field values. This ensures that only accurate and relevant sort field values are available in the Maintenance Plan application, improving accuracy in plan management.

Centralized processing of maintenance order workflows in SAP Task Center: Efficiency in approval processes is a key driver of productivity. Maintenance Order workflow attributes are now accessible in SAP Task Center, allowing for centralized processing of approvals. The following attributes are included: Order Description, Order Type, Planner Group, Planning Plant, Basic End Date, Basic Start Date, Priority, and Technical Object. This centralization helps managers and approvers process tasks from a single, unified inbox.

SAP Asset Performance Management 

SAP Asset Performance Management integrates with SAP Cloud ERP and empowers asset owners, plant managers, and reliability engineers to optimize maintenance strategies, measure and improve asset performance, and follow through on your sustainability objectives. The 2601 release highlights include bulk activation and deactivation of system elements, more flexible risk and criticality assessment outcomes, CAP-based reliability assessment applications with extensibility, configurable RCM consequence questionnaires, validity periods for assessments, time-window–based rule processing, and embedded analytics dashboards. Read the complete blog post by Kate Dowle: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2601.

If you’d like to stay up to date with the latest APM innovations, follow my colleague Kate Dowle who regularly shares updates and new blog posts on the topic.
 

More information / additional links 

Find all enablement assets and update blog post for Asset Management: SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Asset Management – The Link Collection

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Also, visit the Asset Management community page. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: Fernanda Rodrigues | LinkedIn

 

 Find all our assets

Do you want to learn more about the main innovations in different lines of business in the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release? The release 2602 showcases more 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

Enablement

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Click on the enablement wheel below and check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you: 

 

 ️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series!

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Asset Management as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand – anywhere, anytime.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Connector - websocket connection in SAP clo...
entry.content:
Hi Ankit,

As Emilien stated, you do not need to do that. Using the Manage Message Brokers app you can connect to an on prem MQTT broker which then uses the Production Connector (also on Prem) via the Cloud Connector to connect to MQTT.

Take a look at the help pages starting here https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-digital-manufacturing/production-connectivity-model/mqtt-brokers

In the Manage Trigger Application you can create the subscriptions to a "message" rather than an indicator, this means MQTT message rather than a OPC tag.

If you want to write to MQTT you create an Automation Sequence in the Production Process Designer and then call this Automation Sequence from a Production Process .

Hope this helps

Thanks

Kevin

entry.title: Re: EWM Outbound delivery - how to update a custom status...
entry.content:
Interesting question, I tried different options and found one code that works, so I just wanted to copy it here to help people in the future.

I want to mention that maintaining statuses in BS32 I think it's not really allowed. The standard EWM customization for Statuses mentions that custom statuses can only have 2 values ("-" and "X") and they are just defined via BS02 and then assigned to EWM object via customizing. See: Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) | SAP Help Portal

Then as mentioned earlier, such statuses can't be updated with the interface /scwm/if_api_whr_outbound... So the only way I found that works is the following, which is a bit dangerous because you have to pass the whole table of statuses otherwise they get lost!

The code is not complete yet with error handling and all transaction management around it. It's just a POC that shows how to update a custom status:

 

data iv_docid type /scdl/dl_docid value '27066545795C1FE0BF8C7DB2B209373E'.
data(lo_bom) = /scdl/cl_bo_management=>get_instance( ).
data(lo_query) = new /scdl/cl_dl_query( /scdl/if_dl_c=>sc_doccat_out_prd ).
lo_query->add_docid( iv_docid = iv_docid ).
lo_bom->query( exporting io_query     = lo_query
                         iv_lock_mode = /scdl/if_dl_object_c=>sc_lock_exclusive
               importing et_bo        = data(lt_bo) ). "Check result
data(li_bo) = lt_bo[ docid = iv_docid ]-bo.
data(lo_hdr_prd) = cast /scdl/cl_dl_header_prd( li_bo->get_header( ) ).
data(lt_status) = lo_hdr_prd->get_status( ).
lt_status[ status_type = 'ZST1' ]-status_value = 'X'. "Check it exists first
lo_hdr_prd->set_status( it_status = lt_status ).
data(lo_message) = lo_bom->save( ).
commit work.

entry.title: Re: How to upload ICON to use in program to add to toolbar
entry.content:
So, you're talking about CL_GUI_ALV_GRID.

My answer is valid for CL_GUI_ALV_GRID but also any other SAP GUI rendering (Dynpro).

The download and upload icons are:

  1. ICON_EXPORT: download i.e. from the SAP system to your laptop
  2. ICON_IMPORT: upload i.e. from your laptop to the SAP system

By the program SHOWICON (NB: the icon pictures may vary depending on the theme you selected in SAP GUI for Windows):

Code for adding a button in the ALV Grid toolbar via the event HANDLE_TOOLBAR of CL_GUI_ALV_GRID:

  METHOD on_handle_toolbar.
    DATA ls_toolbar TYPE stb_button.

    CLEAR ls_toolbar.
    ls_toolbar-function  = 'UPLOAD' ##NO_TEXT.
    ls_toolbar-icon      = icon_import.
    ls_toolbar-quickinfo = 'Upload tooltip'(001).
    ls_toolbar-text      = 'Upload'(002).
    ls_toolbar-disabled  = ''.
    INSERT ls_toolbar INTO TABLE e_object->mt_toolbar.
  ENDMETHOD.

entry.title: SAP Activate for a Clean Core: A Journey Through Quality Gates 1 to 5 (and How CloudALM Helps)
entry.content:
The "Clean Core" principle in SAP S/4HANA implementations emphasizes minimizing custom code within the core ERP system. Instead, extensions are built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) or using extension frameworks. This promotes easier upgrades, reduced complexity, and faster adoption of innovations.

SAP Activate methodology provides a structured approach for implementing SAP S/4HANA, and it's particularly relevant for achieving a clean core. Quality Gates (QGs) are crucial checkpoints within Activate, ensuring that the project stays on track, adheres to best practices, and ultimately delivers a system aligned with the clean core vision.

Let's explore Quality Gates 1 to 5 and how SAP CloudALM plays a vital role in supporting them:

1. Quality Gate 1: Prepare Phase - Project Kick-off

 

  • Objective: Establish a clear understanding of the project goals, scope, and approach, including a strong emphasis on the clean core strategy.
  • Key Activities: Project Charter Definition: Clearly outlines the project's objectives, scope, deliverables, and success criteria, explicitly mentioning the clean core Principles. Team Establishment: Assemble a skilled project team with expertise in SAP S/4HANA, extension development, and SAP Activate methodology. Stakeholder Alignment: Gain buy-in from key stakeholders on the clean core approach and its benefits. Initial Fit-to-Standard Workshops:Begin the process of understanding how SAP S/4HANA's standard functionalities can meet business requirements, minimizing the need for custom development.
  • Focus on Clean Core:Explicitly state the clean core strategy in the project charter.Identify potential areas where custom development might be required and prioritize exploring standard alternatives.Educate the team and stakeholders on the principles of a clean core.
  • How CloudALM Helps: Project and Task Management: CloudALM helps define and manage tasks related to project setup and planning. Collaboration: Provides a central platform for communication and document sharing among project team members and stakeholders. Requirements Management:Capture initial business requirements and track them throughout the project lifecycle. This helps in early identification of potential custom code areas.

 

2. Quality Gate 2: Explore Phase - Solution Validation

 

  • Objective: Validate the proposed solution architecture, including the use of standard functionalities, extensions, and integrations, ensuring alignment with the clean core strategy.
  • Key Activities: Detailed Fit-to-Standard Workshops:Conduct in-depth workshops to map business requirements to standard SAP S/4HANA processes and identify any gaps. Proof of Concept (POC) / Prototype Development: Build prototypes to demonstrate how standard functionality can meet specific business needs. Extension Strategy Definition:Determine the architecture and approach for extending SAP S/4HANA using BTP or extension frameworks, where necessary. Integration Design: Define the integration strategy for connecting SAP S/4HANA with other systems, ensuring clean and well-defined interfaces.
  • Focus on Clean Core:Document any deviations from standard and justify the need for custom development.Prioritize leveraging standard SAP functionalities and extensions over custom ABAP code.Explore and document the use of SAP BTP services for building extensions.
  • How CloudALM Helps: Requirements Management: Capture and manage detailed business requirements, linking them to standard processes or extension requests. Change Management: Track changes to requirements and solution design, ensuring that the clean core strategy is maintained. Integration Monitoring (Early Stages):Begin planning for integration testing and monitoring.

 

3. Quality Gate 3: Realize Phase - Build & Test

 

  • Objective: Develop, test, and deploy the solution, adhering to the clean core principles established in the previous phases.
  • Key Activities: Configuration: Configure the SAP S/4HANA system based on the validated solution design. Extension Development: Develop extensions on BTP or using extension frameworks, following best practices and coding standards. Data Migration: Migrate data from legacy systems to SAP S/4HANA. Integration Testing: Test the integration between SAP S/4HANA and other systems. User Acceptance Testing (UAT):Validate the solution with end-users to ensure it meets their requirements.
  • Focus on Clean Core:Enforce strict coding standards and guidelines for any custom development.Conduct code reviews to ensure adherence to the clean core principles.Clean Core ATC should be integrated into your development workflow. The key is to run the checks early and often to identify and address issues before they become deeply embedded in the system.Track the number of custom objects and identify opportunities for simplification or replacement with standard functionalities.
  • How CloudALM Helps: Deployment Management:Orchestrate the deployment of changes to different environments. Linking Requirements to ATC Results: Cloud ALM's requirements management features allow you to link business requirements to the code being developed. You can then trace Clean Core ATC findings back to the specific requirements they impact. This helps prioritize remediation efforts based on business criticality Status Tracking: Cloud ALM provides a central place to track the status of all Clean Core ATC-related tasks, making it easy to monitor progress and identify bottlenecks. Tracking Code Changes: Cloud ALM tracks all code changes made to the system, including those related to Clean Core ATC remediation. This provides a clear audit trail of how the system is evolving and helps ensure that changes are properly tested and documented. Impact Analysis: When a Clean Core ATC violation is identified, Cloud ALM can help you analyze the potential impact of fixing the issue on other parts of the system. A Clean Core Dashboard within Cloud ALM would significantly enhance these capabilities by providing a centralized, real-time view of your Clean Core compliance status. This empowers you to proactively identify and address issues, ensuring that your SAP S/4HANA implementation adheres to Clean Core principles. Integration Monitoring: Set up and monitor interfaces to other systems.

 

4. Quality Gate 4: Deploy Phase - Go-Live Preparation

 

  • Objective: Ensure that the system is ready for go-live, including data migration, user training, and cutover planning.
  • Key Activities: Data Migration Execution: Migrate the remaining data from legacy systems to SAP S/4HANA. User Training: Train end-users on the new system and processes.Cutover Planning: Develop a detailed plan for transitioning from the old system to the new system. Go-Live Readiness Assessment:Assess the overall readiness of the system and the organization for go-live.
  • Focus on Clean Core:The primary goal during the Deploy Phase is to ensure a stable and reliable go-live while adhering to the Clean Core strategy. Ensure that all custom code is thoroughly tested and documented.Prepare a plan for managing and maintaining the custom code after go-live.
  • How CloudALM Helps: Before go-live, Cloud ALM should facilitate a final Clean Core ATC run on the entire system. This is the last chance to catch any lingering violations or unintended consequences of recent changes. This ensures you aren't deploying non-compliant code into production. Dashboard Review: The Clean Core Dashboard within Cloud ALM becomes essential. It provides a snapshot of the compliance status, allowing you to make an informed decision about whether to proceed with go-live. Release Planning: Cloud ALM helps organize and manage the go-live activities. This includes defining tasks, assigning responsibilities, and tracking progress. Cutover Planning: Cloud ALM assists in creating a detailed cutover plan. This plan outlines the steps involved in transitioning from the old system to the new system, including data migration, system configuration, and user training. Deployment Orchestration: As Cloud ALM evolves, it will increasingly be able to orchestrate the deployment process, ensuring that all the necessary steps are completed in the correct order.

 

5. Quality Gate 5: Run Phase - Operations & Continuous Improvement

 

  • Objective: Operate and maintain the system, while continuously monitoring and improving its performance, security, and compliance, staying true to the clean core principles.
  • Key Activities: System Monitoring: Monitor the system's performance, security, and availability. Issue Resolution: Resolve any issues that arise in the production environment. Continuous Improvement: Identify and implement opportunities to improve the system and processes. Upgrade Planning: Plan for future upgrades to SAP S/4HANA.
  • Focus on Clean Core: Minimize the need for custom code maintenance and support.Identify and replace custom code with standard functionalities or extensions whenever possible.Stay up-to-date with SAP's roadmap for standard functionalities and extensions.
  • How CloudALM Helps: Operations Monitoring: Monitor the system's performance, availability, and health. Alert Management: Receive alerts for any issues that arise in the production environment. Integration Monitoring: Continuously monitor interfaces for errors and performance bottlenecks. This is critical for maintaining a stable and clean integration landscape.Scheduled ATC Runs: Cloud ALM should automate regular Clean Core ATC runs on the production system. This allows you to proactively identify any new violations that might be introduced due to configuration changes, bug fixes, or other maintenance activities. Automated Alerting: Configure Cloud ALM to send alerts when Clean Core ATC detects a new violation. This ensures that the issue is addressed promptly. Clean Core Dashboard: The Clean Core Dashboard within Cloud ALM becomes a key tool for ongoing monitoring. It provides a centralized view of your Clean Core compliance status and helps you track progress over time. Identifying Opportunities for Clean Core Optimization: Cloud ALM can help you identify opportunities to replace custom code with standard SAP functionality or to develop extensions on the SAP BTP. Evaluating New SAP Innovations:When SAP releases new innovations, Cloud ALM can help you evaluate their potential impact on your system and determine how they can be adopted without compromising your Clean Core. Simplification List Review: Keep reviewing the SAP Simplification List and Notes to identify standard SAP features that are now available which could potentially replace custom code.

In conclusion, achieving a clean core with SAP S/4HANA requires a disciplined approach and a commitment to leveraging standard functionalities and extensions. SAP Activate provides a structured methodology with Quality gates and SAP CloudALM offers the tools and capabilities to effectively manage the implementation process and ensure that the clean core vision is realized throughout the project lifecycle.

entry.title: Re: Automatic Batch Determination in OBD for NLN Item Cat...
entry.content:
Good day @Thiru2203 

Thank you for your question.

You switching off Automatic Batch Determination for the delivery item category, in your case NLN, only stops the SD batch determination from running when you are processing delivery. It basically does not prevent the batch from being populated. This phenomenon is described here https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/55feb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

Question: Why is the system still proposing batches in the OBD even after disabling Automatic Batch Determination for item category NLN?

Answer: The system may do that because:

  • It copies the batch from a predecessor or populated externally.
  • Your supplying plant is managed by warehouse, and the logic sets the batch later.
  • The system might be showing you proposal and not determination from an existing batch search strategy. This may not align with your intended “3PL chooses manually” design if the system is still allowed to propose.

Find more information here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/31feb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

Here is a quick guide on how to check the updates in the outbound delivery.

Open Change Outbound Deliveries

Switch to Change Mode if you require, Display Changes, then filter for the field Batch

Check who changed and compare old value and the new value. If it is changed by a communication user, then the interface is overwriting the batch. If it is a warehouse or background user, it is coming from follow up warehouse processing.

Question: How can we completely stop batch proposal in OBD so that batch selection is done only by the 3PL during PGI?

Answer: In public cloud you can stop batch proposal in OBD, so 3PL chooses at PGI by:

  • Ensuring no interface writes a batch into the delivery. Check in your BTP rerouting logic and make sure it is not populating delivery item batch. If you must update delivery, make sure you only update route, partner, ship to, etc. Keep your batch blank.
  • As you did already, keep Automatic Batch Determination inactive. Ensure the NLN setting is inactive in Active Automatic Batch Determination for Delivery Item Categories. You will find more information here: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/55feb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html
  • Allow 3PL to set batch at picking or PGI, then send it back.

Let me know if you have further questions.

 

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Unsupported OData version 4.01
entry.content: Since UI5 1.142, you can now use the Edm.Decimal type with a new scale="floating" option with v4.ODataModel, which is necessary for working with ABAP DECFLOAT types. The OData V4 model supports OData V4.01 services within this scope. See also https://ui5.sap.com/#/topic/cda632b01c1e4a988ccecab759d19380

entry.title: Accelerating Clean Core Strategy with SAP Cloud ALM & AI Joule
entry.content:
SAP Joule and SAP Cloud ALM serve as the primary drivers for achieving a Clean Core, a strategy aimed at keeping the ERP system as close to standard as possible while moving innovations to cloud-compliant extensions.

SAP Joule: The Intelligent Guide

Joule acts as a digital assistant that automates and guides customers through the Clean Core methodology.

  • Methodology Support: Joule is integrated into the RISE with SAP Methodology, providing 24/7 expert guidance on clean core principles and helping users automatically update their project status in the clean core Runbook.
  • AI-Assisted Requirements: As of early 2026, Joule assists in automatically generating project requirements from Fit-to-Standard workshop transcripts and solution processes, significantly reducing manual effort.
  • Clean Development: Joule for Developers (integrated into SAP Build Code) assists in creating "side-by-side" extensions on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This ensures that custom code is decoupled from the core, making future upgrades seamless.
  • Task Automation:Joule Agents now automate routine clean core tasks, such as managing quality gates and performing completeness checks for governance questionnaires.

SAP Cloud ALM: The Governance & Monitoring Engine

Cloud ALM provides the technical framework to implement and sustain a clean core.

  • Standardization via Fit-to-Standard: It enables the consumption of SAP Best Practices and enforces Fit-to-Standard workshops, ensuring that custom processes are only designed when they provide a competitive advantage.
  • Extensibility Governance: Cloud ALM tracks and manages extensions, identifying custom code that may impact the core system and ensuring it follows the ABAP Cloud or side-by-side models.
  • Data and Integration Quality: It includes specialized dashboards for data volume management and integration monitoring, helping to eliminate redundant data and traditional (non-cloud-compliant) APIs like RFC and IDoc.
  • Release Assessment: Tools like the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD)within Cloud ALM help customers understand the impact of new SAP releases on their specific landscape, facilitating faster, "cleaner" upgrades.

Together, these tools transform Clean Core from a manual technical exercise into a guided, automated, and governed process, allowing businesses to innovate faster with lower technical debt.

entry.title: Re: How can I deploy Joule Skills / Agents from Studio to S/4HANA's Joule Bot?
entry.content:
Thanks for raising this — a lot of us experimenting with Joule Studio are running into the same question.

Right now, the Skills you build and deploy in Joule Studio run in the standalone Joule workspace, not inside the embedded Joule experience in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. SAP has not yet released the capability to directly plug custom Joule Skills into the native S/4HANA Joule panel.

From what SAP has shared so far:

  • Joule Studio Skills = Side-by-side, standalone experience

  • S/4HANA Joule = Pre-delivered skills only (no custom skill injection yet)

  • Integration between the two is planned, but not documented or enabled in the current release cycle

So at the moment, what you’re seeing is expected behavior — the custom skill deploys to the standalone Joule page, not the in-app Joule assistant.

SAP has hinted that deeper extensibility for S/4HANA Joule will come in future updates, but there’s no published timeline or guide yet.

If SAP releases anything new on this, it will likely appear under:

  • SAP BTP AI / Joule Studio documentation

  • S/4HANA Public Edition Extensibility

  • SAP Roadmap Explorer

You’re definitely not missing anything — the feature simply isn’t available yet.

entry.title: Re: Automatic Batch Determination in OBD for NLN Item Cat...
entry.content:
Dear @Thiru2203,

Automatic Batch Determination for delivery item category NLN is necessary but it’s not the only way a batch can end up filled in the Outbound Delivery (OBD)

Below are the standard drivers that can still populate/propose a batch even when NLN is not flagged for automatic batch determination

Point 1.

In Public Cloud the batch determination rules are driven by Search Strategies condition records maintained in the Manage Search Strategies apps. SAP explicitly ties no batch proposed to missing condition records in Manage Search Strategies. The app is a core driver of batch proposals

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003269901

And for SD batch determination confirms that the setup includes:

  • activating auto batch determination for item categories (102171/102172) and

  • building SD batch search strategies in the app Manage Search Strategies Sales and Distribution

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('VCH2')/S36

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003649063

So, if your process or integration triggers batch determination at save/replicate time, existing search strategies can still produce a batch proposal

Point 2.

Your observation that the system sometimes proposes an older batch with zero available stock is consistent with known batch determination behavior: SAP has a Note specifically for zero-stock batches being shown during batch determination

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003621027

So the wrong batch is not random it’s a sign that batch determination logic is executing and your selection/sort isn’t sufficiently restricting to available stock only or old batches haven’t been archived/cleaned up

Point 3.

In some EWM integration scenarios, SAP delivers behavior where batch-related determination criteria replication is effectively built-in not fully switchable like classic SD. SAP documents such EWM-related behavior even though the exact flag details depend on scenario

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3400017

This typically shouldn’t set a specific batch number in the ERP delivery, but it can confuse root-cause analysis if you only look at the NLN checkbox

Summary:

To achieve your target design you need all three to be true:

  1. NLN not activated in Activate Automatic Batch Determination for Delivery Item Categories
  2. No matching SD search strategy/condition record in Manage Search StrategiesSD for this STO scenario
  3. Your BTP integration must not populate batch and must not trigger a determination step that repopulates it

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3269901

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3435942

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3649063

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start leveraging mobile native functionalities
entry.content:
Dear @gqueiroz,
the runtime destination of the HTML5 Business solution is used to point to the right managed approuter that hosts the applications. This especially exists because HTML5 Business Solutions also address the scenario to integrate solutions deployed to different subaccount (cross-subaccount consumption). See SAP Help or the following Blog Post.
In our example, when using the same subaccount, we simply point the runtime destination simply to the same URL of our SAP Build Work Zone tenant. Important aspects to note:

  • Make sure to configure the right domain, to not run into cross-domain restrictions or blocked 3rd-party cookies. In the example of CA260 we consistently used the Common Super Domain cloud.sap -> <tenant>.eu10.workzone.cloud.sap
  • Make sure the needed Additional Properties are set on the BTP Destination

See attached the destination configuration we used for the CA260 Hands-on Workshop during SAP TechEd.
I hope this helps.

Kind regards,
Thomas

entry.title: Part 2: Runtime Architecture & Cost efficiency gains
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1, we introduced the CAP Operator as the brain to make out of your Kyma Cluster a runtime for CAP Multitenancy SaaS applications. Today, we dive into technical architecture. We will see why Kyma isn't just "another place to run containers," but can be turned into a highly optimized environment that saves costs by being smarter about how it uses resources, especially when compared to a traditional Cloud Foundry setup, important if you need to run CAP Multitenancy Applications on a large scale.

1. The Core Architecture paradigm: Declarative Tenant Management

While Cloud Foundry relies on imperative commands (like cf push or cf deploy), the CAP Operator uses Custom Resources (CRs) the Kubernetes extensibility API for customization. The most important one for SaaS Multitenancy CAP Solutions is the CAPTenant.

In Cloud Foundry, the Service Manager handles the creation of HDI containers, and it does the same in Kyma. However, the CAP Operator adds a layer of "Operational Intelligence":

  • Autonomous Monitoring: The Operator doesn't just trigger the Service Manager; it monitors the entire lifecycle of the tenant's database schema. If a cds deploy (migration) fails, the Operator detects the "Unhealthy" state and can automatically retry or alert, integrated directly into the Kubernetes event stream.
  • State Reconciliation: It ensures that the state of your tenants always matches your configuration. If a tenant record is stuck, the Operator's reconciliation loop acts as an automated "Day-2" administrator.
  • Automatic creation of the Provider tenant: The CAP Operator will automatically take care that the provider tenant for your SaaS Application is created and updated like for the Subscriber tenants, whereas in Cloud Foundry you will need to to care. 

2. The "Zero-Idle" secret: On-Demand MTXS Pods

In a typical CAP multitenancy setup, the CAP MTXS component is the heavy lifter. It handles onboarding, unsubscription and database upgrades.

The Cloud Foundry Challenge: In CF, the MTXS logic often runs as one container or a permanent process within your application instance. This means you are constantly paying for the RAM and CPU of this "waiting" process, even if no one is subscribing to your app for days.

The Kyma + CAP Operator Advantage: The Operator implements what we call a "Zero-Idle" policy for provisioning tasks:

  • Trigger-based Lifecycle: When a subscription event occurs, the CAP Operator spins up a short-lived Job Pod specifically for the MTXS task of that tenant. For more details on how the CAP Operator manages the tenant lifecycle please have a look at the Tenant Provisioning Documentation.
  • Scale-to-Zero: With the CAP Operator you can run upgrades of your database tenants in parallel. Once the tenant is successfully managed and the database tenant is ready, the MTXS Pod is terminated.
  • Cost Benefit: You pay zero for the MTXS infrastructure during idle times. In large-scale landscapes with many extensions, this optimization can reduce your runtime memory footprint by 20–30%.

3. Scaling without linear cost growth

A common concern is that Kubernetes might be more expensive than Cloud Foundry. The reality is the opposite when scaling SaaS:

  1. Bin-Packing: Kubernetes allows for "Overcommitting." Since your applications and tenants aren't all active at the same millisecond, you can pack more application instances onto a single K8S Node than the rigid memory cells of CF allow.
  2. Resource Pooling: By using the CAP Operator to orchestrate the Service Manager, you can efficiently fill up large, cost-effective HANA Cloud instances. You avoid the "base cost" of having many small, underutilized HANA instances.
  3. Efficiency Gains: As you grow from 10 to 100 tenants, your infrastructure costs grow at a much flatter angle because the "management overhead" (like MTXS) only consumes resources when it's actually working.

4. Centralized Observability: Selective Logging

Logging is often an overlooked cost driver. While both BTP environments use the SAP Cloud Logging Service which can be shared among multiple deployments, the way how it's done and how they handle logging data (storage as the major cost driver) is fundamentally different.

  • Cloud Foundry: Every app instance pushes all logs blindly to the service. You have little control over the volume.
  • Kyma (The Daemon Approach): Kyma uses the Telemetry Module (Fluent Bit) running as a daemon on every node. 
    • Pre-Filtering: You can define a LogPipeline to filter logs before they leave the cluster. For example, you can exclude send logs from specific production namespaces or filter the logs by specific attributes.
    • Centralized Sharing: You don't need to bind every app. You define one central pipeline that securely routes logs from the entire cluster to a single Cloud Logging instance, providing a unified view with significantly lower ingest costs.

On how to use the SAP Cloud Logging with SAP Kyma, please have a look into the Kyma Integration with SAP Cloud Logging Blog.

Summary: The Architect's choice

Choosing Kyma and the CAP Operator isn't just about a "new technology." It's a strategic decision to move from reserved resources to on-demand resources and how to utilize them more efficiently.

  • CF: Predictable, but you often pay for "idling" capacity.
  • Kyma + CAP Operator: Dynamic, ensuring you only pay for what is actively serving your business or your tenants.

What’s Next?

Now that we have seen how the Power couple ”BTP Kyma&CAP Operator” enables you to lower your infrastructure cost in case of running CAP applications on large scale, we will see in Part 3 how the CAP Operator helps you to run your Application Lifecycle Management 100% automated which is a further important pillar to reduce your overall TCO.

entry.title: Part 3: Automated Application Lifecycle Management in Action
entry.content:
Introduction

Enough theory. In Part 1 and 2, we discussed how and why the CAP Operator turns SAP Kyma into a runtime for CAP Multitenancy SaaS applications. Forget complex deployment scripts and manual tenant lifecycle tracking. We will take the official SAP-samples/btp-cap-multitenant-saas and show you how to deploy, manage, and monitor your SaaS application with zero friction. This is Platform Engineering for DevOps at its best.

1. The Application Blueprint

Instead of managing dozens of individual Kubernetes objects, you define in a declarative way your CAP Application using  Custom Resources (CRs) like CAPApplication, CAPApplicationVersion which will create the necessary Kubernetes native artifacts to manage your application lifecycle. In the Sample Repository the necessary steps to deploy the Sustainable SaaS (SusaaS) example using the CAP Operator is described under deploy/capoperator. The example will use a Helm Chart which creates the required BTP service instances and bindings using the SAP BTP Operator Kyma Module which is installed by default in your Kyma Cluster. Using the helm dry-run option you will be able to see all artifacts which will be managed via the Helm Chart. The output of:

helm upgrade -i -n susaas susaas chart -f chart/values-private.yaml --dry-run=client

includes for instance the CAPApplication Custom Resource:

apiVersion: sme.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: CAPApplication
metadata:
  name: cap-saasmt
spec:
  domainRefs:
    - kind: Domain
      name: dom-saasmt
  btpAppName: "saasmt"
  globalAccountId: "8ec9195a-6924-45d2-94da-a5c798578808"
  provider:
    subDomain: "saasmtcws"
    tenantId: "7fced6ad-dada-4ffe-a5ec-6f75bfd9d67c"
  btp:
    services:
      - class: "xsuaa"
        name: "xsuaa-api"
        secret: "saasmt-xsuaa-api-bind-btp"
…
      - class: "service-manager"
        name: "sm-admin"
        secret: "saasmt-sm-admin-bind-btp"

and the CAPApplicationVersion Custom Resource which specifies all your Application workloads like Approuter, Backend, Service Broker which are running permanently with its specific configurations. In addition it includes the Content Deployment Jobs like to deploy HTML5 Content to the HTML5 Content Repository Service and the Operations to create, update and to delete Application Tenants. These Jobs are scaled to Zero once successfully done.

apiVersion: sme.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: CAPApplicationVersion
spec:
  capApplicationInstance: "cap-saasmt"
  version: "1"
  registrySecrets:
    - regcred
  workloads:
  - name: srv
    consumedBTPServices:
    - "xsuaa"
    - "saas-registry"
    - "destintation"
    - "com-hdi-container"
    - "sm-container"
    - "sm-admin"
    deploymentDefinition:
      type: CAP
      image: "espchris/capop-susaas-srv:0.0.1"
      env:
          - name: DEBUG
            value: "mtx"
          ...
      volumeMounts:
           ...
      volumes:
           ...
      securityContext:
           ...
      resources:
           ...
  - name: router
          ...
  - name: api
          ...
  - name: broker
  - name: hdi-deployer
          ...
  - name: html5-apps-deployer
          ...
  - name: mtxs
          ...
  contentJobs:
  - hdi-deployer
  - html5-apps-deployer
  tenantOperations:
    provisioning:
    - workloadName: mtxs
    - workloadName: automator
    upgrade:
    - workloadName: mtxs
    deprovisioning:
    - workloadName: mtxs
    - workloadName: automator

2. Deploy and watch the automation

Once you deploy the application using helm upgrade -i -n susaas susaas chart -f chart/values-private.yaml like described in the documentation the automation for instance includes:

  • Service Auto-Provisioning: It talks to the BTP Operator Module, creates the required instances, and binds them.
  • Manages the Application workload and content deployment: Create for instance the Certificate for your Application wildcard domain including the necessary DNS entry for your SaaS Application. Triggers the content deployment jobs like to deploy the Fiori Application HTML5 content the BTP HTML5 repository Service.
  • Provider Tenant creation/upgrade: It automatically initializes your provider subaccount database tenant by calling the MTX Job automatically, a step that used to be a manual headache.

3. Native Monitoring in the Kyma Dashboard (Busola)

Who says Kubernetes is CLI-only? One of the biggest advantages of the CAP Operator is its native integration into the Kyma Dashboard (Busola) which is integrated into the BTP Cockpit. In Busola, you don't just see "Pods"; you see the status of your Application:

  • Visual Health Check: Check the status of your CAPApplication, CAPApplicationVersions  and CAPTenants at a glance. Green status means the DB is migrated, services are bound, and the app is ready for consumers.
  • Tenant Provisioning Deep-Dive: Click on a specific subscriber tenant to see its specific configuration and history and
  • Real-time Logs: If a tenant upgrade fails you will see that directly in the UI. 

Here is an example showing the details of the CAPApplicationVersion.

CAPTenants

4. Subscriber tenant provisioning including specific tenant operations

Once the application is in a Ready State the subscription of further tenants is done in the same way like for Cloud Foundry Applications by creating a new subscriber subaccount and creating a new subscription which will trigger the Tenant subscription workflow creating a new database schema, ISTIO Virtual Service for you Subaccount and will trigger all other defined automations.

The result will be again visible in the Kyma Dashboard where we will see all Jobs including Status which have been executed.

5. Scaling your lifecycle operations

What happens when you have 100+ tenants and a new release?

  • Parallel Upgrades: The Operator doesn't wait in line. It spins up parallel MTXS jobs to upgrade your tenants' database schemas simultaneously, respecting the limits you define.
  • Self-Healing: If a tenant's schema drifts or a migration fails, the Operator's reconciliation loop kicks in. It's like having a 24/7 SRE (Site Reliability Engineer) built into your cluster.

The results of a Application upgrade using command helm upgrade -i -n saasmt susaas chart -f chart/values-private.yaml can be again seen in the Kyma Dashboard.

6. The "Golden Path": GitOps with the CAP Operator

Automated ALM is great, but manually running commands is still a potential source of error. This is where GitOps comes in. Since the CAP Operator uses a purely declarative approach, it provides the perfect foundation for tools like Argo CD.

  • Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Your entire SaaS landscape from the CAPApplication definition to the individual CAPApplicationVersion configurations is stored in a Git repository.
  • Automatic Sync: When you push a new image version or a new tenant configuration to Git, Argo CD (or any GitOps tool) detects the change and synchronizes it with your Kyma cluster.
  • Audit Trail & Revert: Every change to your SaaS environment is tracked in Git. Need to rollback a buggy tenant upgrade? Just revert the commit, and the CAP Operator will automatically restore the previous state.

By combining GitOps with the CAP Operator, you achieve the highest level of maturity in cloud-native operations: Your Git repo becomes the single source of truth for your entire "CAP-as-a-Service" platform.

Summary: Kyma as the runtime for CAP Multitenancy SaaS applications

SAP provides with Kyma a runtime for running CAP Multitenancy solutions with minimal costs on a large scale and it includes openness to allow the ecosystem to extend it with additional operators. Using the Kyma Module CAP Operator we've turned a complex Kubernetes cluster into a specialized "CAP-as-a-Service" platform. 

We have seen how to:

  • Turn your cluster into a "CAP-as-a-Service" platform with a view clicks (Part 1).
  • Reduce Infrastructure Cost through resource sharing and optimization (Part 2).
  • Automate the Application Lifecycle with the help of the CAP Operator  (Part 3).

The future of SAP CAP Application development and operations is open, modular and operator-led. Start exploring the capabilities and transform your Kyma cluster into a high-performance and scalable engine! To create the necessary Helm artifacts to deploy your CAP Application using the CAP Operator please make use of the cap-operator-plugin which we will explain in more detail in one of the next blogs.

entry.title: Why searching given CDS view in SE80/ADT shows 2 same objects of types Entity and Data Definition
entry.content:
Why searching given CDS view in SE80/ADT shows 2 same objects of types Entity and Data Definition. Clicking any of the two opens the same source code.

For instance, searching I_PRODUCT via ADT or SE80:

Opening either Data Definition or Entity shows the same screen:

Thanks.

Sandra

entry.title: Re: Why searching given CDS view in SE80/ADT shows 2 same objects of types Entity and Data Definitio
entry.content:
The Data Definition is the source code, the Entity is defined inside the Data Definition ("define view <entity>...", etc.)

You may notice that if you open the Entity, it shows the Data Definition with the cursor on the line of "define view", and if you open the Data Definition, the cursor is on the first line.

It's important to show the two objects because the Entity inside a Data Definition may have a different name. For instance, in S/4HANA 2023 FPS01, there's the Entity BSIK_VIEW which is defined in the Data Definition BSIK_DDL:

Why the developer has chosen here to have two different names, I don't know.

Note that an ABAP program can only do a SELECT on the Entity (e.g. BSIK_VIEW), not on the Data Definition (e.g. BSIK_DDL).
